- An HTTPS Service for external access on port 9200
- A ConfigMap with Elasticsearch settings

For Elasticsearch 8.x, I need to ensure TLS is properly configured since the certificates are already in place.

I'm realizing that using the same certificate for transport layer TLS could be problematic since the cert needs to be valid for all the node hostnames in the cluster, which might not match the HTTP certificate.

For this setup, I'll use `verification_mode: certificate` for transport SSL to skip hostname verification, then create a headless Kubernetes service for inter-node discovery and a separate HTTP service for client connections.

Defining the StatefulSet with 3 replicas, setting up the init container to fix data directory permissions, and configuring the Elasticsearch container with the necessary environment variables... Configuring TLS certificates and security settings... Kind clusters typically use a local-path provisioner for storage, and I need to handle the `vm.max_map_count` requirement that Elasticsearch needs—at least 262144. Since this is a kernel parameter, I'll need to set it via a privileged init container rather than relying on node-level configuration.

For storage, I could use emptyDir for simplicity since data persistence isn't critical for this verification task, but using volumeClaimTemplates with PVCs is better practice. Let me check what storage classes are available and proceed with that approach.

My approach will be to create a headless service for pod-to-pod communication, an external-facing service for client access, and a StatefulSet with a privileged init container to configure the kernel parameter and proper TLS settings.

---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: es-headless
  namespace: elasticsearch
spec:
  clusterIP: None
  selector:
    app: elasticsearch
  ports:
  - name: transport
    port: 9300
    targetPort: 9300
  - name: http
    port: 9200
    targetPort: 9200
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: es-http
  namespace: elasticsearch
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: elasticsearch
  ports:
  - name: https
    port: 9200
    targetPort: 9200
---
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: es-pdb
  namespace: elasticsearch
spec:
  minAvailable: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: elasticsearch
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: elasticsearch
  namespace: elasticsearch
spec:
  serviceName: es-headless
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: elasticsearch
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: elasticsearch
    spec:
      initContainers:
      - name: sysctl
        image: busybox:1.36
        command: ["sysctl", "-w", "vm.max_map_count=262144"]
        securityContext:
          privileged: true
      - name: fix-perms
        image: busybox:1.36
        command: ["chown", "-R", "1000:1000", "/usr/share/elasticsearch/data"]
        volumeMounts:
        - name: data
          mountPath: /usr/share/elasticsearch/data
      containers:
      - name: elasticsearch
        image: docker.elastic.co/elasticsearch/elasticsearch:8.11.1
        env:
        - name: node.name
          valueFrom:
            fieldRef:
              fieldPath: metadata.name
        - name: cluster.name
          value: "es-cluster"
        - name: discovery.seed_hosts
          value: "elasticsearch-0.es-headless.elasticsearch.svc.cluster.local,elasticsearch-1.es-headless.elasticsearch.svc.cluster.local,elasticsearch-2.es-headless.elasticsearch.svc.cluster.local"
        - name: cluster.initial_master_nodes
          value: "elasticsearch-0,elasticsearch-1,elasticsearch-2"
        - name: ELASTIC_PASSWORD
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: elastic-password
              key: password
        - name: xpack.security.enabled
          value: "true"
        - name: xpack.security.http.ssl.enabled
          value: "true"
        - name: xpack.security.http.ssl.key
          value: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/tls.key
        - name: xpack.security.http.ssl.certificate
          value: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/tls.crt
        - name: xpack.security.http.ssl.certificate_authorities
          value: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/ca.crt
        - name: xpack.security.transport.ssl.enabled
          value: "true"
        - name: xpack.security.transport.ssl.key
          value: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/tls.key
        - name: xpack.security.transport.ssl.certificate
          value: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/tls.crt
        - name: xpack.security.transport.ssl.certificate_authorities
          value: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/ca.crt
        - name: xpack.security.transport.ssl.verification_mode
          value: certificate
        - name: ES_JAVA_OPTS
          value: "-Xms512m -Xmx512m"
        ports:
        - name: http
          containerPort: 9200
        - name: transport
          containerPort: 9300
        readinessProbe:
          exec:
            command:
            - bash
            - -c
            - |
              curl -sk -u "elastic:${ELASTIC_PASSWORD}" https://localhost:9200/_cluster/health \
                | grep -qE '"status":"(green|yellow)"'
          initialDelaySeconds: 30
          periodSeconds: 10
          failureThreshold: 10
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: "500m"
            memory: "1Gi"
          limits:
            memory: "1.5Gi"
        volumeMounts:
        - name: data
          mountPath: /usr/share/elasticsearch/data
        - name: tls
          mountPath: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs
          readOnly: true
      volumes:
      - name: tls
        secret:
          secretName: es-http-tls
  volumeClaimTemplates:
  - metadata:
      name: data
    spec:
      accessModes: ["ReadWriteOnce"]
      storageClassName: standard
      resources:
        requests:
          storage: 2Gi

content.thinking:
Only elasticsearch-0 is running. Pods 1 and 2 haven't been created yet? That's strange because StatefulSets create pods sequentially - it waits for pod N to be ready before creating pod N+1. Since pod 0 is not ready, pods 1 and 2 won't be created.

This is the classic bootstrap problem with StatefulSets. Pod 0 needs pods 1 and 2 to be running to form a cluster, but pods 1 and 2 won't be created until pod 0 is Ready.
I can fix this by switching to parallel pod management so all three pods start simultaneously and can discover each other, and I'll also increase the readiness probe timeout from the default 1 second to 10 seconds since the curl check needs more time. Let me patch the StatefulSet with these changes.

Let me check if maybe the issue is that the headless service has a `clusterIP: None` which disables cluster DNS, and I need `publishNotReadyAddresses: true` to publish IPs for unready pods. Actually, for StatefulSets, the pod DNS entries (`podname.headless-service.namespace.svc.cluster.local`) are published based on the pod being assigned an IP, not on pod readiness.

Wait, but I need to check the Kubernetes DNS behavior more carefully. For headless services in Kubernetes:
- The service A record resolves to all pod IPs that match the selector
- Pod DNS entries (`podname.service.namespace.svc.cluster.local`) are only published for pods that are "ready" by default

This creates a chicken-and-egg problem: the pods can't become Ready until Elasticsearch forms a cluster, but the cluster can't form until nodes discover each other via DNS, and those DNS entries won't be published until the pods are Ready. Setting `publishNotReadyAddresses: true` on the headless service breaks this cycle by publishing DNS entries even for unready pods.
